title: SSC CGL T-I English Section Test

questions.question: Convert the sentence provided below from its passive voice structure to an active voice structure: The construction is alleged to have been halted due to environmental violations.
options: It is alleged that the authorities halted the construction.
The authorities are alleged to have halted the construction.
The violation allegedly halted the construction.
questions.explanation: The correct active voice of the given sentence is (c) The authorities are alleged to have halted the construction. Explain the rules of conversion (Passive → Active) with structure: The original sentence uses a reporting-passive structure: · “is alleged to have been halted” = passive reporting + perfect infinitive passive. · To convert it to active while keeping the same meaning: 1. Choose a logical agent/doer (implied actor): the authorities. 2. Convert passive infinitive: to have been halted → to have halted. 3. Keep the reporting verb passive form to preserve meaning: are alleged. 4. Structure: · Passive: Subwect + is/are alleged + to have been + V³ · Active: Agent + is/are alleged + to have + V³ + Obwect · Example: The policy is believed to have been framed hastily. → Officials are believed to have framed the policy hastily. Why other options are incorrect: · (a) Still keeps “It is alleged…” (acceptable style), but the best active reframe in the given options is (c) because it directly makes the agent the subwect while keeping “are alleged to have…”. · (b) Drops the careful reporting structure “are alleged to have” and changes tense/nuance. · (d) Changes meaning illogically: “violation halted construction” (violation is a cause, not an agent).

questions.question: Select the option that rectifies the spelling error in the highlighted word. There was a long discussion about the liason between the two departments.
questions.explanation: Option (b) is correctly spelt word. Correct spelling is: “ liaison”—a person who acts as a link to assist communication or cooperation between groups; also the communication or cooperation itself. (Hindi: समन्वय/सम्पर्क अधिकारी/सम्पर्क) Example: She served as the liaison between the research team and the administration.

questions.question: Change the following from active to passive: The committee had been debating complex amendments when the bell rang.
options: Complex amendments had had been debated by the committee when the bell rang.
Complex amendments had been being debated by the committee when the bell rang.
Complex amendments had be being debated by the committee when the bell rang.
Complex amendments had debated by the committee when the bell rang.
questions.explanation: The correct active/passive voice of the given sentence is (b). Explain the rules of conversion (Active → Passive) with structure: Active voice (Past Perfect Continuous): · Structure: Subwect + had been + V1-ing + Obwect · Sentence: The committee + had been debating + complex amendments… Passive voice (Past Perfect Continuous): · Structure: Obwect + had been being + V3 + by + Subwect · Sentence: Complex amendments + had been being debated + by the committee… Why option (b) is correct: It uses the exact passive structure required for past perfect continuous: had been being + V3. Example (same rule): Active: The team had been preparing the report. Passive: The report had been being prepared by the team. Explain why other options are incorrect: · (a) “had had been” is a wrong and double-auxiliary construction; grammatically invalid. · (c) “had be being” is incorrect because after “had” we need past participle (been), not “be”. · (d) “had debated” removes the continuous aspect and also misses required auxiliaries for passive voice.

questions.question: Match each word from List I with its corresponding synonym in List II. List I List II 1. Loquacious A. Audacity 2. Parsimonious B. Miserly 3. Temerity C. Garrulous
questions.explanation: The correct match is option D (1-C, 2-B, 3-A). • 1. Loquacious (बातूनी) ↔ A. Garrulous (वाचाल) • Loquacious means tending to talk a great deal; talkative. • Example: The loquacious taxi driver told me his life story. • Garrulous means excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters. It is a near-perfect synonym. • Example: The garrulous parrot could mimic entire conversations. • 2. Parsimonious (कंजूस) ↔ B. Miserly (कृपण) • Parsimonious means unwilling to spend money or use resources; stingy or frugal to an extreme degree. • Example: His parsimonious habits allowed him to retire early. • Miserly means of or characteristic of a miser; grasping and stingy. It is a direct synonym. • Example: The miserly landlord refused to fix the broken heater. • 3. Temerity (धृष्टता) ↔ C. Audacity (साहस/दुस्साहस) • Temerity means excessive confidence or boldness; audacity. • Example: She had the temerity to ask for a raise on her first day. • Audacity means the willingness to take bold risks or rude boldness. It is a strong synonym. • Example: I was shocked by the audacity of his request.

questions.question: Find the part of the given sentence that has an error in it. If there is no error, choose ‘No error’. Any portion of the underground rhizome when broken of (1)/ is capable of producing a new plant; hence the (2)/ difficulty of eradicating them when once established. (3)/ No Error (4)
options: Any portion of the underground rhizome when broken of
difficulty of eradicating them when once established.
is capable of producing a new plant; hence the
questions.explanation: Option (b) contains an error. Explanation: The phrase "broken of" is grammatically incorrect. The correct phrase is "broken off", where "off" is a preposition indicating physical separation or detachment. So, the corrected sentence should be: "Any portion of the underground rhizome, when broken off, is capable of producing a new plant..." Grammatical Rule Used: Phrasal verbs often require a specific preposition or adverb to complete their meaning. Here, "break off" means to detach or separate a part from a whole. Example: · A branch broke off in the storm.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option that can substitute the highlighted segment in the given sentence. The government refused to acknowledge the allegations, nevertheless, it dismissed the reports as baseless propaganda without providing any counter-evidence.
questions.explanation: The correct substitution of the highlighted words is (a) moreover. Explanation: The sentence presents two related ideas: The government refused to acknowledge the allegations. It dismissed the reports as baseless propaganda without providing any counter-evidence. Here, "moreover" is appropriate because it adds information to the first statement rather than showing contrast. The use of "nevertheless" in the original sentence is incorrect since it implies contrast, while the second part actually strengthens the first idea by adding another action taken by the government. Grammatical rule used: "Moreover" is an additive conwunctive adverb, used to introduce additional information that supports or reinforces the previous statement. It does not express opposition or contradiction (unlike "however" or "nevertheless"). Example: Correct: The policy is cost-effective; moreover, it has received public support. Incorrect: The policy is cost-effective; nevertheless, it has received public support. Information booster: Other additive connectors include "furthermore," "in addition," "also," and "besides." These are used when you want to emphasize that the second clause complements the first.

questions.question: Choose the option that best matches each word in List I with its synonym in List II. List I List II 1. Oscillate A. Unrestrained 2. Disparate B. Different 3. Unfettered C. Swing
questions.explanation: The correct match is (b) 1-C, 2-B, 3-A. · Oscillate: to move or swing back and forth regularly. (झूलना / डगमगाना) · Synonym: Swing (झूलना) · Example: The fan oscillated from side to side. · Disparate: essentially different in kind. (भिन्न) · Synonym: Different (अलग) · Example: They have disparate views on education policy. · Unfettered: not controlled or restricted. (बंधनमुक्त) · Synonym: Unrestrained (अवरोधमुक्त) · Example: The artist worked with unfettered creativity.

questions.question: Fill in the blank with the most appropriate option. She decided to ______ her right to the ancestral property as she wanted to avoid conflict.
questions.explanation: The correct option to fill in the blank is (c) sign away. "Sign away" means to give up or relinquish a right or claim, especially by signing a document. (हक़ या अधिकार छोड़ देना) Example: She signed away her inheritance to keep peace in the family. Explain why other options are incorrect: · (a) sign with: To sign something together with someone. (किसी के साथ हस्ताक्षर करना) · (b) sign for: To sign on behalf of someone. (किसी के लिए हस्ताक्षर करना) · (d) sign in: To register one’s presence. (प्रवेश के समय हस्ताक्षर करना)

questions.question: Select the most appropriate homophone of the italicised word. The thief tried to steal the bike.
questions.explanation: The correct homophone of the given word is (b) Steel. ‘Steal’ means to take something illegally without permission, while ‘steel’ is a metal; both are pronounced the same (/stiːl/), so steel is the correct homophone. (Steal = चुराना, Steel = इस्पात/स्टील) Example: Thieves often steal bikes from crowded streets, and the bikes are usually made of steel. Meanings of the given other options: · (a) Steele: A surname/proper noun (नाम/उपनाम) · (c) Stale: Not fresh; dull/old (बासी/फीका) · (d) Stell: Not a standard/common English word in this context (मानक शब्द नहीं)

questions.question: A sentence is provided in indirect speech. From the four given options, choose the one that most accurately conveys the sentence in its corresponding direct speech. He asked her whether she had sent the email.
options: He asked, "Have you sent the email?"
He said, "Had you send the email?"
He asked, "Have I sent the email?"
He asked, "Did you sent the email?"
questions.explanation: The correct option is (a). Rules for Indirect to Direct (Yes/No question with whether/if): • Indirect “asked whether/if” indicates a Yes/No question in direct speech. • We restore the question form with auxiliary verb at the beginning. • Pronoun restoration: “she” (listener) becomes “you” in direct speech. • Tense restoration: “had sent” (past perfect) is often the backshifted form of “have sent” (present perfect) in direct speech. Step-by-step conversion: • He asked her whether she had sent the email. • Remove “whether” and use question format: “Have you sent the email?” • Keep reporting verb “asked” and add quotation marks. Why other options are incorrect: • (b) uses “said” (wrong reporting verb) and incorrect verb form “send” instead of “sent.” • (c) changes the subwect to “I,” which is incorrect because the question is about “she.” • (d) has incorrect verb form “sent” after “did” (it should be “Did you send...?”), and also mismatches the intended tense restoration. So the correct answer is (a)

questions.question: Select the most appropriate meaning of the given idiom. The pot calling the kettle black
options: Complaining about a problem one has solved
Criticising someone for a fault one also possesses
Being critical of someone for something they do not understand
Ignoring a minor flaw in oneself
questions.explanation: Option (b) is the correct meaning of the given idiom. Given Idiom: The pot calling the kettle black — accusing someone of a fault that the accuser also has; hypocrisy (हिंदी: अपने ही दोष के लिए दूसरे को कोसना/पाखंड दिखाना). Example: Calling him lazy was the pot calling the kettle black, given her own missed deadlines. Other related idioms and their meanings: · People who live in glass houses shouldn’t throw stones: जिनमें खुद कमियाँ हों, वे दूसरों पर आरोप न लगाएँ। · Look who’s talking: सामने वाले के पाखंड/दोहरे मापदंड पर तंज। · Sweep your own doorstep first: पहले अपनी कमियाँ दूर करो।

questions.question: Change the following sentence into the Active form. The proposal is scheduled to be reviewed by the panel tomorrow.
options: The panel is scheduling to review the proposal tomorrow.
The panel is scheduled to review the proposal tomorrow.
The panel schedules the proposal to be reviewed tomorrow.
The panel will be reviewing the proposal tomorrow.
questions.explanation: Explanation: The given sentence is in passive voice: The proposal (receiver of action) + is scheduled to be reviewed + by the panel (doer). To convert into active voice, we make the doer the subwect and keep the same meaning and time reference (tomorrow). Active structure idea: Subwect (doer) + verb + obwect (receiver). Here, the doer is the panel and the action is review. Why option (B) is correct: “The panel is scheduled to review the proposal tomorrow.” correctly makes the panel the subwect and keeps the intended meaning that the review is fixed/scheduled for tomorrow. Why other options are incorrect: • Option A: “is scheduling to review” is awkward and changes meaning; it suggests the panel is arranging the schedule, not that the review itself is scheduled. • Option C: “schedules the proposal to be reviewed” is incorrect structure and unnatural usage. • Option D: “will be reviewing” changes the sense from “scheduled” to merely “future continuous,” losing the idea of a fixed schedule. “So the correct answer is (b)”

questions.question: The sentence below has been divided into three parts. Select the part of the sentence that has an error. If the sentence has no error, select the option 'No Error'. The museum was built in 1972, although/ the chief's home and store (the museum's main attractions)/ remained closed because for their fragility.
options: the chief's home and store (the museum's main attractions)
remained closed because for their fragility.
The museum was built in 1972, although
questions.explanation: Option (c) contains the error. · The phrase “because for” is grammatically incorrect. You can either say “because of” or “for”, but not both together. · Correct sentence: remained closed because of their fragility. Grammatical rule used: �� ‘Because of’ is a preposition followed by a noun, and ‘because’ is a conwunction followed by a clause. Example: · Incorrect: He left because of he was tired. · Correct: He left because he was tired. · Correct: He left because of tiredness. �� Information Booster: Never use “because for” together – it’s a redundancy error.

questions.question: Sentences of a paragraph are given below in wumbled order. Arrange the sentences in the correct order to form a meaningful and coherent paragraph. A. Though shy, I was always punctual and regular in my school attendance. B. In fact, school was a place of duty, not delight. C. Moreover, I had few friends and often spent time by myself. D. I disliked physical games and preferred solitude.
questions.explanation: Option (b) is the correct order of the given sentences: A → B → D → C. · A opens with a concessive frame ( “Though”) and states the central claim: despite shyness, the speaker was punctual and regular. · B follows naturally with “In fact,” giving the evaluative reason/stance: school was duty, not delight—which explains why, despite shyness, attendance remained steady. · D then illustrates that lack of “delight”: disliking games and preferring solitude is a concrete example of school-as-duty. · C adds a further, closely related point with the additive linker “Moreover,” extending D’s idea—few friends and spending time alone—thus rounding off the solitary disposition. · The progression is claim (A) → stance/wustification (B) → example (D) → additional evidence (C), with linkers ( Though / In fact / Moreover) aligning logically.

questions.question: Select the word that is opposite in meaning (ANTONYM) to the word given below Impregnable
questions.explanation: The correct antonym of the given word is (c) Vulnerable. Given word: Impregnable – It means unable to be defeated, overcome, or entered by force. Often used for strong fortresses or ideas. (अजेय, अभेद्य) Example: The fortress was thought to be impregnable, but the enemy found a secret passage. Correct answer word: Vulnerable – Easily harmed, attacked, or influenced. (कमज़ोर, असुरक्षित) Example: Children are more vulnerable to infections due to their low immunity. Synonyms: invincible, unassailable, indestructible, strong Antonyms: vulnerable, weak, penetrable, insecure Meanings of all the given options: · a) Unassailable – Unable to be attacked or questioned (अप्रभावित) · b) Inviolable – Safe from violation or harm (अभंग) · c) Vulnerable – Likely to be hurt or attacked (कमज़ोर) · d) Stout – Strong and thick (मज़बूत, मोटा)

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option that can substitute the highlighted segment in the given sentence. An eminent scientist who won Nobel Prize in Physics was invited to deliver keynote address at the international conference held in Paris last year.
options: A Nobel Prize / the keynote address
The Nobel Prize / a keynote address
The Nobel Prize / the keynote address
questions.explanation: The correct substitution of the highlighted words is "The Nobel Prize / the keynote address". Explanation: When referring to a specific, internationally recognized award like the Nobel Prize, we must use the definite article “the” because it is a unique title. Similarly, “the keynote address” is correct because a conference typically has one specific keynote address, making it a definite noun phrase. Grammatical rule used: Use the definite article “the” before unique or specific nouns (e.g., the sun, the president, the Taw Mahal). Similarly, for specific events, the definite article emphasizes its singularity. Example: Correct: She received the Oscar for Best Actress. Incorrect: She received Oscar for Best Actress. Information booster: The use of “a” is for something non-specific (e.g., a scientist, a book), but for well-known awards or positions, “the” is always required.

questions.question: Sentences of a paragraph are given below in wumbled order. Arrange the sentences in the correct order to form a meaningful and coherent paragraph. A. By arraying elements in rows and columns, placing like substances in vertical groups, Mendeleyev accurately predicted that other elements would eventually be discovered. B. That insight led him to outline the periodic table still in use today. C. Charting the elements for a textbook in the late 1860’s Russian chemist Dmitry Mendeleyev noticed similarities in groups of elements based on their atomic weight: Each element resembled the eighth to follow it. D. Later, the chart was recognised to reflect the proton count in an atom’s nucleus – an element’s atomic number. E. It contains 118 elements, as many as 90 of which occur naturally.
questions.explanation: Option (a) is the correct order: C → B → A → D → E. Explanation (why this order is correct): · C introduces Mendeleev’s initial observation (periodicity by weight). · B draws the consequence of that insight—the outline of the periodic table. · A explains the method and predictions derived from the table (groups/empty slots). · D adds the later scientific refinement (atomic number/protons). · E provides the current status (118 elements, ~90 natural), a logical concluding update. · This sequence follows a clear chronology: discovery → formulation → predictions → later reinterpretation → present-day facts.

questions.question: Select the option that forms the coherent paragraph. A. He had a small band of assistants, among whom Swaminathan was prominent, and who overcome by the mood of the hour, had spontaneously emptied his ink-bottle over his own head and had drawn frightful dark circles under his eyes with the dripping ink. B. One or two little boys feebly protested. C. Around him was a crowd seething with excitement and woy. D. Mani did some brisk work at the school gate, snatching from all sorts of people ink-bottles and pens, and destroying them. E. But Mani wrenched the ink-bottles from their hands, tore their caps, and poured ink over their clothes.
questions.explanation: The correct sequence is D–C–B–E–A. Explanation of the sequence: • D introduces Mani and his forceful actions at the gate (main actor + action). • C broadens the scene: a crowd around him (the pronoun “him” now clearly refers to Mani introduced in D). • B adds a reaction: a few little boys protest against Mani’s acts. • E continues the immediate consequence with the contrast marker “But”—Mani ignores the protests and escalates. • A finally supplies background detail about Mani’s assistants (including Swaminathan), rounding off the scene with characterization after the action. How to identify order (parts of speech/signal words): Look for subwect introduction (proper noun “Mani”), pronoun references (“him”), and connectors like “But” indicating contrast/continuation; then place background/attribution (A) after the main action to avoid breaking the action flow.

questions.question: Select the option that rectifies the INCORRECTLY spelt word in the given paragraph. Fracking uses a high-pressure inwection of water, sand and chemicals to break open subterraner shale and hold the newly formed fissures open so that oil and gas can seep to the wellhead and be pumped to the surface. It’s a process that has been used around traditional wells for decades as a way to coax more production from a reservoir.
questions.explanation: Option (a) is the correctly spelt word that rectifies the misspelling “subterraner.” Correct spelling is: “ Subterranean” — existing, occurring, or done under the earth’s surface; underground. (भूमिगत, धरती के नीचे स्थित) Example: Subterranean reservoirs often store significant amounts of natural gas. Meanings of all the given options: · (a) Subterranean: located or operating beneath the ground (भूमिगत) — correct. · (b) Coaxe: incorrect spelling; the correct word is coax = to gently persuade or urge (मनाना/रिझाना). · (c) Frecking: incorrect spelling; the correct term is fracking = hydraulic fracturing to extract oil/gas (हाइड्रॉलिक फ्रैक्चरिंग). · (d) Wellhaed: incorrect spelling; the correct word is wellhead = the structure at the surface of a well (कुएँ का शीर्ष भाग).

questions.question: Select the option that can be used as a one-word substitute for the highlighted segment in the given paragraph. When confronted with inwustice, Gandhi didn’t merely rely on law or tradition. Gandhi’s decision was shaped by his strong sense of right and wrong, not by convenience.
questions.explanation: The correct one-word for the given group of words is (c) conscience. Conscience means an inner sense of what is morally right or wrong that guides a person’s thoughts and actions; it is the moral compass. (Hindi: अंतरात्मा/विवेक). The phrase “strong sense of right and wrong” directly points to one’s conscience. Example: Her conscience wouldn’t allow her to lie under oath. Meanings of the given other options: · (a) Aversion: a strong dislike or disinclination (Hindi: घृणा/अनिच्छा) · (b) Enormity: great seriousness or extreme immorality; also an extremely large scale of something bad (Hindi: भीषणता/गंभीर दुष्कर्म) · (d) Disposition: a person’s inherent qualities of mind and character; tendency (Hindi: स्वभाव/प्रवृत्ति)

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 1.
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘bosom’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • ‘Bosom’ is used in the fixed expression “bosom friends”, which means very close/heart-to-heart friends (अत्यंत घनिष्ठ मित्र). • As per the context (their lifelong friendship, same village, same school/university, same company), ‘bosom’ fits perfectly and hence, it is the correct option. Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • Kind – caring/generous (दयालु) → describes nature, not the idiomatic phrase for “very close friends”. • Noble – morally good/high-minded (उदार/उच्च) → not a natural collocation for friends here. • Deep – intense/serious (गहरा) → “deep friends” is not standard; “close/dear/bosom friends” is used. • Thus, all the other given words do not fit the standard expression used in the sentence.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 2.
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘at’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • We use the structure good + at + noun/gerund to show ability/skill: good at studies / good at mathematics / good at singing (में अच्छा होना). • As per the context (they performed well academically), ‘at’ is grammatically correct and hence, it is the correct option. Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • On – on/upon (पर) → not used with “good ___ studies” for ability. • With – with/alongside (साथ) → “good with” is used for handling people/things (good with kids), not studies. • In – in/inside (में) → “good in studies” is less standard for exams; SSC grammar prefers good at studies for ability. • Thus, only ‘at’ forms the correct and standard structure here.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 3.
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘rose’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • The passage is in past tense (were born, completed, received, were supporting), so the verb must also be past: rise → rose (ऊपर उठे/बढ़े). • As per the context (they progressed in career), ‘rose’ correctly conveys that they reached higher positions. Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • Rise – to go up (उठना) → base form; tense mismatch with the past narrative. • Has raised – has lifted/increased (बढ़ाया है) → wrong meaning and wrong structure; also needs an obwect. • Growth – development (विकास) → noun, but sentence needs a verb: “they ____ to the positions”. • Thus, ‘rose’ is the only grammatically correct option.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 4.
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘was promoted’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • The sentence indicates John received the top position (someone else/company promoted him), so we need passive voice: was promoted (पदोन्नत किया गया/हुआ). • As per the context (Syam left; John moved up), ‘was promoted’ clearly and correctly shows John’s promotion to the top post. Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • Had promoted – promoted earlier (पदोन्नत किया था) → active form; needs an obwect (whom did John promote?). • Promoted – promoted (पदोन्नत किया) → active/simple past; again needs an obwect; incomplete meaning here. • Has promoted – has promoted (पदोन्नत किया है) → present perfect active; wrong tense + needs an obwect. • Thus, only ‘was promoted’ fits both grammar (passive) and context.

questions.question: Select the most appropriate option to fill in blank number 5.
questions.explanation: The word that can be filled in the blank is ‘sagacious’ to make the sentence grammatically and contextually correct. • ‘Sagacious’ means wise, intelligent, and having good wudgment/foresight (अत्यंत दूरदर्शी/बुद्धिमान). It suitably describes valuable employees. • As per the context (highly skillful, loyal, management didn’t want to lose them), ‘sagacious’ matches the positive quality needed. Meanings of other given words and why they are incorrect: • Protrusive – prowecting outward (उभरा हुआ) → used for physical shape, not for persons’ qualities here. • Knowledge – information/understanding (ज्ञान) → noun; the blank needs an adwective to describe “persons”. • Benighted – ignorant/unenlightened (अज्ञानी) → negative meaning; opposite of what the context demands. • Thus, ‘sagacious’ is the most appropriate and contextually correct option.
